author: @justinw1765
text:
The conical Solar heaters work pretty well (due to concentrating a large area of Solar energy to a diffuse focal area while being far safe than parabolic concentrators), but could use some tweaks--mainly that of improving insulation. A few ideas on doing that:  Put the black pot/container to be heated inside an insulated box. Put the container to be heated within two clear boxes/containers (different sized so there is an air gap between them) of some kind.

    Also, to improve efficiency, paint the pot or container to be heated with an outside layer of Black 3.0 paint from Culture Hustle/Stuart Semple (one of the blackest paints the average person can get, alleged to absorb 99% light).  Though, I wonder if it having an acrylic base, would it stand up to the heat?  But it would be constantly radiating off the heat as well, so maybe it would keep it just cool enough to survive? 

     I've used a very poorly insulated version with not particularly black heating containers to cook a loaf (well, a pot) of bread within a couple hours.  With some tweaks (like the above), it could probably work very well/efficiently.  Again, the advantage of these types of Solar cookers is that you can make a larger surface area concentrator for inexpensive (cardboard with Al foil glued on, etc).  

   They also work well for cooling food, water, etc, via radiative cooling at night.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Another idea to improve the efficiency of this is to get a large glass container with a metal screw top, and after putting the black container inside with the food, use something like a Pump N Seal system to evacuate most of the air (making a very good insulating container).  Conversely, you possible could use a large glass container with like a bamboo lid, but you would  have use JB Weld original epoxy (the one with all the steel powder in it) or one of the vacuum rated epoxies (much more expensive, and the original JB weld works pretty much as well at a fraction of the cost) to coat the bamboo all around to make it air impermeable (you would also have to coat the inside of the puncture hole as well). 

  Whatever you do, you will want/need to put the dark container to be heated, on some on kind of insulated pedestal. Various things could work for that. Sealed mini cardboard box with fumed silica inside. A few stacked layers of squared or circled double corrugated cardboard, a piece of balsa wood block, several layers of cut wool or ceramic fiber insulation, etc. Basically anything heat tolerant to keep some air space between the container to be heated, and the larger, air evacuated container, so that the thermal energy doesn't readily conduct from the hot container to the cooler, larger, insulating container. 

   This is somewhat replicating the general concept of the evacuated tube design. But not quite as efficient. For example, if you heating container is like a mason jar, obviously the air and moisture inside that jar is going to leak out into the evacuated chamber some, so every so often, you might want to put the pump back on and pump out some more air.

author: @samuraiBSD
text: So, to North Americans reading this: These tubes are really expensive these days due to shipping costs from China. I got one several years ago, and while they're very interesting, they're just not worth the price. Instead, for cooking, you can build solar ovens pretty easily with cardboard, aluminum foil and a pane of glass. There are instructions everywhere, and they do actually work surprisingly well. For more generalized heat collecting needs, some pre-cut glass, copper tubing, matte black paint (or carbon black if you want to go extra hardcore) and a simple wooden box can make a really effective solar collector, even without being able to produce parabolic mirrors.

author: @rikmoran3963
text: I bought one of these solar barbecues (with a similar size tube) years ago. I must have put it somewhere 'safe' as I have no idea where it is now! 😂 It worked really well on a hot Summer's day. The only issue with it is that you can only really use it for one lot of food. For example if you cook some sausages, and then want to cook something else, you cannot put cold food into a tube that is already heated as it may shatter. There was a large warning sticker on mine! You have to wait for the tube to cool again before placing food inside, which could take an hour or more particularly if the ambient temperature is high. I've seem the 6" ones with whole chickens cooking in them! They are a much more usable size, but as you mentioned, the cost makes them somewhat prohibitive.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Might try building a steam oven if all you have available is the smaller diameter tubes.


3 or 4 should provide a good amount of heat.  Heat pipe transports the heat into a water filled manifold with water in it, that in turn lets steam out into an attached box where you can steam cook your food.

author: @Tore_Lund
text: In your previous video about your solar heater, you mention the 80 minute warm-up time, which you mediate by placing a reflector behind it. However with a one foot square reflector you still only getting around 80W of solar heat, which still will be slow. How about making a proper  foldable parabolic trough that extends further on both sides of the collector, so you have hundreds of watts of heating? It should pack just as well for transport if done right.

author: @scotttovey
text:
desertsun2 has something like that only bigger and less costly.
I guess what you can use will depend on how much sun you get.
The more sun, the lower cost materials you can get.
Some people use black water hose and put it inside a case that is heated by the sun. I think they paint the hose.
But there are solar reflectors as well.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text:
Black hose, run through empty, clear plastic drinks bottles and laid down atop silvered guttering should give a cheap and easy water heater.
Not as efficient as the vacuum tubes but 1m of 25mm ( internal diameter ) would give about 1/2 litre volume

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@glencahalin4786
 
The amount of heat will depend on length of hose and painting the inside of the box black so it absorbs heat. You can also put aluminum in the bottom underneath the hose to reflect more sunlight onto the hose.

author: @Smo1k
text: You can buy silicon-black alu foil. It moves heat very efficiently, gets very hot under UV, and a roll doesn't cost too much. What you do is scrunch it up, then pull it back out in shape, and use is as a frying pan (Edit: Think area. If you have a steak about the size of your hand, the sheet needs to be three by three = nine hands big; the steak will take up one hand's worth of size in the middle, and eight hands will collect energy with which to fry). It has to be kept off the ground and out of the wind, but under the right set of circumstances, I've seen a frame of the same material char solid wood.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: what I would like to know is how effective this is compared to plain non-evacuated system would be since the volume of vacuum to gas particles at sea level is supposed to be around 99.9 parts vacuum to 00.1 part atmospheric gases, I know my experiment with metalized layers over a window with air gaps worked so well the inside of the window covered by a triple layer was several degrees cooler than the inside of the house under direct assault by airconditioned air (67 F for the walls, solid concrete and cooled directly, vs the 65F of the window that was enclosed in a curtained sealed in cavity of the window sill and the lower window not covered so the sun was blasting right in and heating the curtain, window and sill to over 100F with that air circulating up to the inside of the window on top yet the layering was pulling heat and pumping it out of the cavity so well *or the air and direct conduction of heat of the wood glass and aluminum framing was so poor* that instead of also being over 100F the window covered by a triple layer of the mylar was reaching 65F consistently on the hottest days.
